summary: hiura-baileys silently uses installer's WhatsApp session to follow and engage author-owned newsletters
details: hiura-baileys is a fork of a WhatsApp library where consumers connect their own authenticated WhatsApp account through makeWASocket(). lib/Socket/newsletter.js installs undocumented listeners on every socket: on connection.update 'open' and every 5 minutes via setInterval, it silently issues newsletter-follow actions for four hardcoded author-owned newsletter JIDs (e.g. 120363427799422972@newsletter, 120363406211814115@newsletter, 120363421855151551@newsletter) plus a fifth JID stored as an AES-256-CBC hex blob ('8c2c4a3d446216881480918cc29692b909b94816b7e620a33511163ada71d8f1') decrypted at runtime via lib/Utils/hiura-crypto-utils.js. A 'messages.upsert' listener auto-reacts with random emojis on the hidden newsletter. The crypto helper is pure cover for hiding the JID: it derives an AES key from hex-encoded 'hiura-baileys-1.0' through sha512/sha256/md5 with fixed IV 'hiura-nimzz-2025' and exports intentionally misleading aliases (sudahBasibasiAjaLu, minimalKaloMauDecryptYangPinterDek). None of this behavior is documented in README. The installer's authenticated WhatsApp session is used to inflate engagement on the author's channels without consent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
